title: Build LLM Apps with LangChain

modules.title: LangChain Architecture: Chains, Prompts, and the Execution Model
modules.sub: Understanding how LangChain wires prompts into executable pipelines
modules.description: Learners map out the core building blocks of LangChain — chains, prompt templates, and the execution model that ties them together. You trace how a request moves through a chain from input to output, building mental models that underpin everything that follows. By the end, you can read and reason about a LangChain pipeline without getting lost.

modules.title: Connecting to Models: OpenAI, Anthropic, and Open-Source Alternatives
modules.sub: Choosing and configuring the right LLM backend for your app
modules.description: Learners configure LangChain to work with multiple model providers — OpenAI, Anthropic, and self-hosted open-source options — and compare their tradeoffs in cost, capability, and latency. You make deliberate decisions about which model fits a given use case and swap providers without rewriting your chain logic. This module establishes provider-agnostic thinking as a core professional habit.

modules.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Embeddings, Vector Stores, and Document Loaders
modules.sub: Grounding LLM responses in your own documents and data
modules.description: Learners build a retrieval pipeline that loads external documents, converts them to embeddings, stores them in a vector database, and retrieves relevant chunks at query time. You make decisions about chunking strategy, embedding models, and retrieval parameters that directly affect answer quality. The module ends with a working RAG chain you can point at your own document sources.

modules.title: Memory and Conversation State: Making Apps Context-Aware
modules.sub: Giving your app the ability to remember what users said before
modules.description: Learners implement different memory strategies — buffer, summary, and entity memory — to make chains aware of conversation history across turns. You evaluate the tradeoffs between memory types and decide which fits your application's context window constraints and use case. By the end, your app can hold a coherent multi-turn conversation rather than treating every message as isolated.

modules.title: Tool Use and Function Calling Inside Chains
modules.sub: Letting your LLM take actions by calling real external tools
modules.description: Learners wire external tools — search engines, calculators, APIs, and custom functions — into chains so the LLM can decide when and how to invoke them. You build an agent loop that reasons over available tools and handles tool outputs before generating a final response. This module is where your app stops being a text generator and starts being an autonomous actor.

modules.title: Structuring Output: Parsers, Validators, and Typed Responses
modules.sub: Turning raw LLM text into reliable, machine-readable structured data
modules.description: Learners apply output parsers and validators to enforce structured, typed responses from LLM calls — moving from freeform text to JSON objects, Pydantic models, and typed schemas. You handle parse failures gracefully and design prompts that make structured output more reliable. The result is application logic that can actually consume LLM output without brittle string parsing.

modules.title: Apply It: Build a Domain-Specific Assistant for Your Own Use Case
modules.sub: Designing and assembling a complete assistant for your professional domain
modules.description: Learners integrate everything from prior modules to design and build a domain-specific assistant scoped to their own professional context — whether that's legal research, customer support, internal tooling, or another area. You make architecture decisions about which LangChain components to combine, what data to retrieve, and what tools to expose. The deliverable is a working, demonstrable application tied to a real problem you own.

modules.title: Deployment, Observability, and LangSmith for Production Debugging
modules.sub: Shipping your app and monitoring what actually happens in production
modules.description: Learners deploy their LangChain application and instrument it with LangSmith to capture traces, debug chain failures, and monitor latency and token usage in production. You interpret trace data to diagnose where a chain broke down and make targeted fixes without guesswork. The module closes with a deployment checklist and observability posture that makes your app maintainable beyond the initial build.

synopsis: LangChain is the dominant framework for connecting LLMs to real-world data, tools, and workflows — and understanding its primitives is now a baseline skill for any professional building AI-powered software. This course moves fast through core concepts and lands on a working, deployable application you design around your own domain.
rationale: LangChain has become the de facto orchestration layer for LLM applications, yet most professionals lack structured knowledge of its architecture, making them dependent on fragile tutorial code they cannot adapt or debug.
learning_outcome: Professionals can design, build, and deploy a functional LangChain-powered application that connects an LLM to external data sources and tools relevant to their own work context.
source_signals: Building a Virtual Machine Inside ChatGPT (score: 4803)
Open source AI is the path forward (score: 5021)
nearest_existing: Magic you understand is a tool.
